coredata.dc:title: Batch diagnosis of batteries within one second

Du, Wenjia
Mager, Bastian
Shearing, Paul R.
Miller, Thomas S.
Jervis, Rhodri
coredata.dc:description: As demand grows for scalable and sustainable energy storage, fast and affordable diagnostics are urgently needed, especially for factory-level cell sorting and second-life assessments. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is widely used but remains too slow, complex, and costly for large-scale use. This work introduces a multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation response (MMER) technique that captures comparable impedance-related information at a fraction of the time and cost. MMER can diagnose entire battery modules in 1 s, regardless of cell count. It uses a binary multi-frequency excitation signal, implemented on simple hardware such as programmable logic devices. Unlike EIS, MMER avoids frequency-domain transformation and impedance fitting. Instead, it compares raw voltage responses under a shared excitation current to reveal performance variations. Experiments show MMER tracks cell health in line with EIS while reducing test time by over 99%. It supports real-time diagnostics even during high-rate cycling and extends to other electrochemical systems.

battery diagnostics
signal processing
inconsistency evaluation
voltage response
multi-channel and multi-frequency
electrical response
excitation response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impedance and multi-frequency electrical excitation response
Multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation response
Impedance and multi-frequency electrical excitation response
Multi-channel electrical excitation response and discharge

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was carried out with funding from the Faraday Institution (faraday.ac.uk; EP/S003053/1 ), grant numbers FIRG060 , FIRG061 , FIRG082 , FIRG085 , and FIRG086 . P.R.S. was supported by the Department of Science, Innovation, and Technology (DSIT) and the Royal Academy of Engineering under the Chair in Emerging Technologies program (CiET1718/59). W.D. and P.R.S. acknowledge the support of the Oxford Martin School .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of individual cells harvested from an end-of-life automotive pack
(A) Individual impedance spectrum for each cell.
(B) Individual multi-frequency voltage response in 1 s for each cell, same perturbation signals with the frequency 2k (k = 1, 2, 3, …, 9).
(C) The first three features projected from impedance spectra via principal component analysis.
(D) The normalized Euclidean distance between the impedance spectrum of each cell.
(E) The normalized Euclidean distance between the individual voltage response of each cell.
(F–H) Locally enlarged voltage response for all cells, in the range of 0.1 s (F), 10 ms (G), and 1 ms (H).
(I and J) The average normalized Euclidean distance for each cell using the impedance spectrum (I) and individual multi-frequency voltage response (J).
Repeatability analysis of voltage responses from cell #1 under consistent and varied perturbation conditions
(A and B) Voltage response of cell # 1 during multiple tests, using the same perturbation signals (A) and different amplitude perturbation signals (B) with the frequency 2k (k = 1, 2, 3, …, 9).
(C and D) Locally enlarged voltage response during multiple tests for the same perturbation signals (C) and different amplitude perturbation signals (D).
(E and F) The Euclidean distance between the multiple tests of cell # 1, same perturbation signals (E) and different amplitude perturbation signals (F).
Comparison of conventional and electrical response-based diagnostic approaches
(A) An illustration of the electrochemical impedance spectrum measurement for the single battery—sequential perturbations of differing frequencies are used to construct a Nyquist plot.
(B) Multi-frequency perturbation current synthesis based on the Walsh function, with the frequency 2k (k = 1, 2, 3, …, 9).
(C) The measurement duration of the individual impedance spectrum, individual voltage response, and multi-channel voltage response.
(D) Comparison of battery evaluation based on individual impedance, multi-channel impedance, individual voltage response, and multi-channel voltage response.
Discharge behavior and multi-channel voltage response analysis of all batteries
(A) The discharge profiles of all batteries at a rate of C/10.
(B) Locally enlarged discharge profiles of all batteries at a rate of C/10.
(C) Multi-channel voltage responses for batteries, perturbation signal with the frequency 2k (k = 1, 2, 3, …, 9).
(D) The Euclidean distance between the multi-channel voltage response of each cell.
(E and F) Locally enlarged multi-channel voltage response for all batteries.

head.ce:title: Batch diagnosis of batteries within one second

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, TUM School of Computation, Information and Technology, Technical University of Munich, Theresienstraße 90, 80333 Munich, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: TUM School of Computation, Information and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, TUM School of Computation, Information and Technology, Technical University of Munich, Theresienstrasse 90, 80333 Munich, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As demand grows for scalable and sustainable energy storage, fast and affordable diagnostics are urgently needed, especially for factory-level cell sorting and second-life assessments. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is widely used but remains too slow, complex, and costly for large-scale use. This work introduces a multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation response (MMER) technique that captures comparable impedance-related information at a fraction of the time and cost. MMER can diagnose entire battery modules in 1 s, regardless of cell count. It uses a binary multi-frequency excitation signal, implemented on simple hardware such as programmable logic devices. Unlike EIS, MMER avoids frequency-domain transformation and impedance fitting. Instead, it compares raw voltage responses under a shared excitation current to reveal performance variations. Experiments show MMER tracks cell health in line with EIS while reducing test time by over 99%. It supports real-time diagnostics even during high-rate cycling and extends to other electrochemical systems.

The rapid growth of battery technologies is central to achieving a net-zero society, but it comes with a major bottleneck: reliable and high-throughput diagnostics to ensure cell consistency across production and reuse cycles. Existing tools like electrochemical impedance spectroscopy offer detailed insights into battery health but are too time-consuming, expensive and operationally complex for widespread deployment, especially at giga-factory scale or in second-life applications. This study introduces a transformative solution: the multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation response (MMER) technique. MMER enables full-module screening of batteries in just 1 s, independent of cell count, with diagnostic accuracy comparable to EIS, but with over 99% reduction in time. It eliminates the need for time-to-frequency domain conversion and instead relies on direct voltage response comparisons using a shared binary excitation signal.

This capability represents a major improvement over conventional methods, unlocking real-time, non-invasive, and scalable battery diagnostics that are suitable not only for manufacturing quality control but also for onboard safety screening, second-life triaging, and operando monitoring. MMER addresses long-standing challenges in the field, such as identifying cell imbalance in serial configurations and diagnosing batteries during dynamic usage without requiring steady-state conditions. Its using field programmable gate array (FPGA) compatibility and hardware simplicity make it particularly attractive for industrial adoption. Continued development of MMER can accelerate the safe deployment and circular use of batteries and other electrochemical systems.

This study presents a fast, low-cost method to diagnose entire battery modules within 1 s. Using simple and shared multi-frequency current perturbation and direct electrical response comparisons, the technique matches the accuracy of the impedance measurement while enabling real-time monitoring during cycling. This supports scalable, efficient, and safer battery manufacturing and reuse.

ce:section.ce:para: Battery technologies are critical for supporting net zero and ensuring energy security. 1 Many battery-powered applications require high energy and power. Thus, several cells are connected in series or parallel to form a module or a pack. 2 For optimal performance, safety, and longevity, these grouped batteries should have a high level of cell-to-cell homogeneity. Otherwise, due to the “bucket effect,” weaker batteries may deplete or overcharge faster than better ones, leading to performance imbalances or safety risks as hazards propagate between neighboring cells. These issues can arise throughout the entire lifespan of a cell, starting with variations in materials and manufacturing processes in newly produced cells. Furthermore, this is also significant for retired batteries, 3 as they have different usage histories, and some need to be recertified into new battery modules/packs. Likewise, for in-service batteries, real-time evaluation is essential for identifying and replacing weaker batteries and preventing further deterioration, 4 as one cell that exhibits open-circuit failure will cause other cells connected to it in series to become inoperable.

The unpredictable nature of battery failure in real-life operating conditions leads to significant variability in failure duration and randomness in critical trigger points. Combined with technical limitations in the type and quality of monitorable data during battery operation, fault diagnosis can be highly challenging. Existing battery fault diagnosis methods are developed for laboratory purposes with the main focus on fault phenomena analysis and mechanism explanation. However, real-world monitoring must consider several factors, such as sensor availability, data quality, and fluctuating operating conditions, often making laboratory-based methods impractical for industry or as onboard diagnostics. Non-destructive techniques, such as neutron imaging or industrial X-ray computed tomography (CT), can provide detailed insights into the battery’s evolution of internal structural changes and lithium distribution during thermal runaway, 5
,
6
,
7 aging, 8
,
9
,
10 and abuse usage 11
,
12 without opening the cell. These tools are either less cost-effective or difficult to access, which hinders large-scale industrial applications. Similarly, regular constant current-constant voltage (CCCV) charging/discharging 13
,
14 to get the overall performance profile is also time-consuming and can cause extra cell degradation during the diagnostic process. Other methods, such as acoustics, 15
,
16
,
17 are also non-destructive but sensitive to both sensor installation and environmental noise, which can affect the accuracy and reliability of the measurements.

Effective fault diagnosis algorithms require in-depth analysis of extensive real-world field data, 18
,
19
,
20
,
21 such as battery management system (BMS) data on voltage, current, and temperature, along with the use of advanced algorithms to continuously estimate and track the state of health (SoH) of a battery. A work by Wang et al. 22 introduced an anti-noise adaptive long short-term memory model for remaining useful life prediction, incorporating multi-feature coordination and feedback correction to address variability in battery performance under changing operating conditions. While these parameters help monitor charge levels and thermal conditions, they are insufficient for a comprehensive assessment, as they do not reveal internal degradation. Recent “data-driven” works 23
,
24 have demonstrated that by using only electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and machine learning (ML), the SoH and remaining useful life of a battery cycled at the same charging/discharging rates as the training cells can be accurately estimated at any point of its life, as well as the discharge capacity. 25
,
26
,
27 In the module level, 28
,
29 internal temperature estimation, 30 and second-life assessment, 31
,
32 EIS is also effective. However, the impedance measurement is time-consuming, as each measurement takes several minutes to cover a broad range of frequencies, and potentiostats with EIS capabilities are costly compared with more basic power supplies, especially when considering a large number of batteries. Impedance features can serve as original diagnostic variables, as impedance is derived from the AC perturbation and corresponding response. Therefore, all the information contained in the impedance is also present in the electrical response, as it acts as the source signal. 33

To address the current limitations of battery diagnostics, a fast and low-cost approach is proposed in this work to enhance battery evaluation for both manufacturing and second-life applications. The multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation response (MMER) technique involves generating a multi-frequency perturbation signal based on the Walsh function, 34 which is passed through multiple batteries. The resulting electrical signature of the cell, either a voltage or current response induced by the perturbation signal, is then measured, and the state of the electrochemical cell is evaluated directly using this electrical signature. Our tool mitigates inconsistencies across multiple test setups and provides reproducible results within 1 s. The scalability and usability make this approach potentially highly efficient for the industry. Furthermore, by integrating signal processing and ML techniques, we can directly analyze excitation responses in the time domain, eliminating the extra steps of EIS. This not only reduces measurement errors and hardware costs but also helps address several critical challenges, particularly the impracticality of using traditional, instrument-based methods at scale, given the large volume of both fresh and retired batteries. For retired batteries that could be significantly degraded or that have unknown usage history, cycling to evaluate them could represent a potential safety risk, so our non-invasive method is designed to deliver reliable assessments without the need for full electrochemical cycling, and therefore without compromising safety. Additionally, our technology enables real-time evaluation while batteries are in operation, avoiding the need for downtime during the operation of lifetime evaluation testing. Finally, our methods can be used reliably onboard operating systems to provide real-time feedback on cell SoH during the lifetime of an application. Specifically, this can serve as a key metric for determining the SoH and expected lifetime of batteries by tracking the initial voltage response and full life cycle performance as part of the battery passport. 35 Here, we demonstrate the ability of the technique to categorize commercial vehicle batteries in 1 s with 100% accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impedance and multi-frequency electrical excitation response
ce:section.ce:para: Before introducing the MMER technique, the suitability of the multi-frequency electrical excitation response (MER) to replace traditional EIS is first verified by applying both methods to the same recycled cells from a commercial Tesla battery pack. The impedance spectra of eight cells from this pack are shown in Figure 1 A. Each impedance spectrum was measured individually, and each battery was discharged to 3 V before measurement to ensure that any observed impedance differences were due to variations between cells rather than fluctuations in state of charge (SOC); further details regarding the methods can be found in Methods. When external factors such as temperature and measurement conditions are controlled precisely, the impedance of the batteries with a similar SoH should be very similar. Considering that the impedance of a battery increases with aging, different impedance values correspond to different SoH. 36 As shown in Figure 1A, the impedance spectra of cells #2, #3, and #5 exhibit a positive shift along the real axis compared with other cells, indicating higher impedance in the high-frequency region (series resistance). The ohmic resistances for these cells are approximately 0.0375 Ω, 0.0368 Ω, and 0.0359 Ω, respectively.

The battery voltage under multi-frequency signal excitation is shown in Figure S1A, which includes both the DC component and the multi-frequency voltage response. Since the lowest excitation frequency is 2 Hz, the 4-s response signal contains four repeated segments, each lasting 1 s. The multi-frequency perturbation signal used in this work is constructed using Walsh functions (Figure S2A only illustrates the method). Fourier transform analysis (Figure S2C) confirms that the energy of the waveform is concentrated at the expected harmonic frequencies. This design provides several advantages 37 over conventional methods that superimpose multiple sine waves (Figure S2B): it delivers significantly higher amplitude at each frequency component (Figures S2D–S2K), resulting in stronger excitation and an improved signal-to-noise ratio in the measured electrical response. Furthermore, as a binary-valued waveform, it aligns well with digital hardware and can be efficiently generated using field programmable gate arrays (FPGAs), allowing for easy integration into fast and compact measurement systems. To ensure the selected frequency components are appropriate for a given electrochemical system, a calibration process using standard EIS can be performed to identify relevant frequency subranges, which then guide the frequency content of the synthesized signal.

To evaluate the similarity between EIS or voltage response curves, the Euclidean distance between two curves can be calculated, where a larger distance indicates greater dissimilarity. The normalized Euclidean distances calculated using multi-frequency voltage responses of different lengths are shown in Figure S3. It can be observed that for a complete cycle (1 s), the repetition has only a negligible impact on the results. For comparisons within a single batch, normalization of the Euclidean distance is not necessary. However, when comparing multiple batches, direct comparison becomes difficult because different batches may exhibit different absolute distance ranges. Without normalization, it is challenging to define a standard threshold for assessing similarity. Therefore, we propose calculating a normalized Euclidean distance matrix to establish a similarity scale from 0% to 100%, enabling intuitive and consistent comparisons across different datasets. Figure 1B illustrates the corresponding multi-frequency voltage responses (in 1 s) measured individually, with the DC component of the cell voltage subtracted, and the individual multi-frequency voltage responses all follow the corresponding EIS measurements. Since the voltage responses are in the time domain with high dimensionality, the locally enlarged view in Figures 1F–1H more clearly highlights the differences in voltage responses among all cells. Particularly, cells #2, #3, and #5 exhibit higher voltage response signals compared with other cells. Since the multi-frequency voltage response inherently captures dynamic behaviors related to impedance, whether it can indirectly reflect SoH through statistical features such as standard deviation (SD) is explored. Although SD is not a direct measurement of impedance, it is influenced by the convolution of multiple-frequency responses. SDs of cells #2, #3, and #5 are approximately 0.0701 V, 0.0677 V, and 0.0666 V. The relationship between the AC voltage response and the excitation current is determined by the impedance of the battery, where the voltage amplitude depends on both the impedance magnitude and current amplitude, and a phase difference exists due to the non-resistive component of the battery impedance. Since the excitation current is identical for all cells, the multi-frequency voltage responses can effectively represent the impedance characteristics of different batteries, including the ohmic resistance, charge transfer, and diffusion process. Therefore, the higher multi-frequency voltage responses of cells #2, #3, and #5 indicate that their impedance is higher than other cells, as the voltage response is determined by all components of impedance and its multi-frequency characteristics, which current interruption entirely misses. The impedance spectra derived from the multi-frequency voltage responses closely match the full EIS spectra, as demonstrated by overlapping Nyquist plots for 18650 retired Tesla model S cells (Figure S4). This quantitative agreement substantiates the claim that multi-frequency voltage responses contain equivalent impedance information to EIS.

Notably, the SD of cell #4 is also relatively large, at 0.066 V. By contrast, the SD of the other cells—#1, #6, #7, and #8—are 0.059 V, 0.059 V, 0.061 V, and 0.058 V, respectively. The impedance of cell #4 is primarily larger in the mid-to-low-frequency range, which is reflected in the upward shift of its impedance spectrum along the imaginary axis. This behavior might be due to the growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer or the cracking of cathode particles. 36 The differences between cells by calculating the Euclidean distances of both the full EIS measurements and the MER are evaluated, confirming that the method can effectively capture cell-to-cell differences. Some cells, such as cell #4, exhibit more significant differences in their EIS spectra at low frequencies, motivating us to test whether extending the frequency range in the MER could enhance this distinction. Specifically, the frequency range was broadened from 2–512 to 1–1,024 Hz, although extending to 1 Hz inevitably increased the measurement time due to the longer periods required at lower frequencies. As shown in Figures S1C and S1D, the lower frequency extension makes the voltage response of cell #4 more pronounced, consistent with its higher low-frequency impedance. However, despite this more noticeable voltage response, the extended frequency range does not yield a significant improvement in the ability to distinguish between cells, as evidenced by the normalized Euclidean distance results shown in Figure S1E.

As seen in the impedance spectrum, inconsistent degradation among cells exists, which can be attributed to differences in cell location or orientation. 8 Therefore, when selecting cells for remanufacturing or reuse, it is crucial to choose those with consistent characteristics. The impedance spectra of consistent cells should overlap at the same SOC. For instance, while cells #2, #3, #4, and #5 all exhibit increased impedance, cells #3 and #5 appear more similar in terms of values and shape. Nevertheless, this is difficult to assess objectively, so a kernel principal-component analysis (PCA) is employed to nonlinearly project the impedance spectrum measured at 41 frequency points onto lower dimensionality to capture and visualize these similarities. The result of this dimensionality reduction is reflected in the feature spaces, as shown in Figure 1C, where cells #3 and #5 are closer in the first three feature spaces after the dimension reduction of the original EIS. Here, each feature space represents a principal component, which captures a certain amount of the variance (information) present in the original high-dimensional data. The first three features typically retain the largest portions of this variance, making them the most representative for distinguishing underlying patterns and similarities between different cells. The similarity is further confirmed by the Euclidean distance on the Nyquist plot, shown in Figure 1D, which illustrates the closeness of their impedance spectra across all frequencies. The diagonal elements represent the distance of a cell to itself (Euclidean distance is 0, indicating complete overlap). For cell #3, the closest cell in Euclidean distance is #5, while the farthest is #8, which aligns with the dissimilarity in the shapes of their impedance spectra, as shown in Figure 1A. Likewise, Figure 1E shows the Euclidean distances calculated based on individual voltage responses, leading to remarkably similar results in the Euclidean distances and therefore the ability to draw the same conclusions as from Figure 1D. However, the measurement time for each impedance spectrum in Figure 1A takes 1.25 min, as the frequency needs to be scanned sequentially from 10,000 to 1 Hz, whereas each multi-frequency voltage response measurement in Figure 1B takes only 1 s. The total measurement time for all 8 cells is thus reduced from ca. 10 min to 8 s, significantly shorter than the time required for even a single impedance spectrum. Figures 1I and 1J present the average normalized Euclidean distances for each cell (calculated from distances between the cell of interest and each other cell in the set). These averages indicate consistent trends between the impedance spectrum and multi-frequency voltage response measurements. Cells #2 and #8 show relatively larger average distances compared with the others, suggesting that they should be excluded when assembling a new battery pack using those cells.

To evaluate the generality and robustness of the MER, its application was extended to a broader range of battery formats and testing conditions, as different battery chemistries exhibit distinct impedance behaviors and degradation pathways, 36
,
38 which naturally influence the MER, as it encodes the same impedance information as EIS. In addition to the 18650-format retired Tesla model S cells analyzed previously, the technique was validated on fundamentally different cells, namely, 21700-format LG M50 lithium-ion batteries. Additionally, different instruments and varied excitation frequency ranges were also tested. As shown in Figure S5, the multi-frequency voltage perturbation consistently reflects the SoH of different cells, with results comparable to conventional EIS, as confirmed by the Nyquist plots again (Figure S6). It is worth noting, however, that the frequency content of the binary excitation signal must be adapted depending on cell chemistry, size, and degradation state to ensure key impedance features are adequately captured.

The sensitivity of the multi-frequency voltage response to noise has been validated by adding 1%, 5%, 10%, and 20% random noise to the original signal (Figure S7). As shown in Figure S7J, the addition of less than 5% white noise has little effect on the relative Euclidean distances between different cells, which is consistent with the results based on the original multi-frequency voltage responses and EIS data. Even with 10% noise, as can be concluded from Figure S7K, for cell #2, the most similar cells (those with the lowest Euclidean distances) are still cells #3 and #5. When the random noise is increased to 20% (Figures S7D and S7H), the cell health information contained in the multi-frequency voltage response is finally lost. This demonstrates the remarkable robustness of the technique to electrical noise.

To verify the repeatability of the multi-frequency voltage response technique, repeated measurements of cell #1 were carried out and are presented in Figure 2 A, with a 60-s interval between each repeated measurement. The relative error in the actual excitation current during each measurement is very small, with an average error of less than 10−6 A (see Figure S8). For the same cell with a constant SOC, the multi-frequency voltage responses from multiple measurements almost completely overlap (Figure 2C), and the normalized Euclidean distances between the measurements are nearly zero (as shown in Figure 2E). These results confirm the high repeatability and reliability of the multi-frequency voltage response measurements under controlled conditions. Figure 2B shows the voltage response when the multi-frequency excitation signal increases from 1.5 to 2 A (details of amplitudes of different excitations can be found in Figure S9). Considering that in a battery pack for an energy storage system, cells are often connected in a mixed configuration, the excitation current may enter different cells with varying amplitudes due to differences in cell impedance. As a result, the voltage response is not solely related to the intrinsic differences between cells. It is also influenced by the amplitude of the excitation current. Therefore, it is important to investigate how the voltage response behavior changes with increasing excitation amplitude. It can be observed that as the excitation signal increases, the voltage response also increases correspondingly. The gradual change in the excitation and the corresponding voltage response is further confirmed by the Euclidean distance between the voltage responses at different excitation levels (Figure 2F). As discussed earlier, although different excitation signal amplitudes lead to varying voltage responses for the same cell (with a specific impedance), the phase information of all voltage responses remains consistent. This observation aligns with the theoretical analysis, and the voltage response at different excitation amplitudes will exhibit only a vertical shift without altering the overall signal shape. These investigations have shown that the use of a combined multi-frequency voltage perturbation can identify a cell’s SoH with as much information as is obtained from the EIS measurements. In addition, the measurements are remarkably consistent with very little variation in repeated tests and are also robust against external environmental factors (such as noise) due to the stable nature of the electrical connection.

To demonstrate the operando capability of MER under load conditions and clarify the steady-state assumptions, experiments were conducted on a high-power INR-21700-P45B cell with a nominal capacity of 4,500 mAh, discharging at 3.5 A, while applying the multi-frequency binary perturbation signal of the same amplitude (Videos S1–S5). In this way, the perturbation signal effectively becomes part of the charging current profile, and separate hardware for perturbation is not required. The current profile and corresponding voltage responses during this operando excitation are shown in Figure S10A and S10B. Over the discharge (Figures S10B and S10C), more than 2,000 individual 1-s multi-frequency voltage responses were collected. The cell temperature remained stable throughout, indicating that the perturbation did not cause significant heating (Figures S10D and S10E). The peak-to-peak values (Figure S10F) and SDs (Figure S10G) of the responses stayed consistent during mid-SOC and increased near the end of discharge, consistent with expected changes in impedance due to factors such as charge-transfer resistance and lithium depletion at low SOC. 39 The lowest response amplitude was observed around mid-SOC, 40 where ion mobility and reaction kinetics are most favorable. Example responses (Figure S10H) taken at the start, middle, and end of discharge further illustrate these changes. Although the battery is not at a steady state during discharge, each 1-s measurement corresponds to less than an insignificantly small change in SOC considering the entire discharge time is more than 3,000 s, allowing the system to be approximated as quasi-steady during each measurement.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Video S1. The real-time voltage response of an INR-21700-P45B cell during operando multi-frequency current perturbation, recorded over a voltage range from ca. 3.85 to 3.72 V
Video S2. The real-time voltage response of an INR-21700-P45B cell during operando multi-frequency current perturbation, recorded over a voltage range from ca. 3.72 to 3.60 V
Video S3. The real-time voltage response of an INR-21700-P45B cell during operando multi-frequency current perturbation, recorded over a voltage range from ca. 3.60 to 3.48 V
Video S4. The real-time voltage response of an INR-21700-P45B cell during operando multi-frequency current perturbation, recorded over a voltage range from ca. 3.48 to 3.30 V
Video S5. The real-time voltage response of an INR-21700-P45B cell during operando multi-frequency current perturbation, recorded over a voltage range from ca. 3.30 to 2.70 V

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation response
ce:section.ce:para: Through the analysis of EIS and multi-frequency voltage responses, it is evident that the multi-frequency voltage response in the time domain encompasses all the frequency information of the impedance in the frequency domain (as illustrated in Figures 3 A and 3B) while drastically reducing measurement time from minutes to seconds. This improvement significantly enhances efficiency and eliminates the need for time-frequency transformations and additional hardware. However, by performing individual measurements, as the number of cells increases, the time required to obtain the multi-frequency voltage response for multiple batteries still increases linearly (as shown in Figures 3C), which is especially important, considering the large-scale production of commercial batteries (1,000 cells per minute for a 5-GWh/annum factory 41 ) and the massive number of batteries that could potentially be involved in second-life applications after retirement. The use of multiple channels for simultaneous acquisition can address this issue. When the batteries are connected in series, a multi-frequency excitation current signal will pass through all the batteries simultaneously, allowing for the simultaneous sampling of the voltage responses of multiple cells by using multiple voltage analyzers. This approach not only allows for the measurement of multiple batteries in one test, thus improving measurement efficiency, but also helps avoid errors and costs introduced during multiple measurements, such as poor contact or inconsistencies in the state of the batteries due to measurements taken at different time scales. The 8 cells studied here are connected in series such that one multi-frequency current perturbation passes through all 8 cells. Using a multi-channel voltage measurement and connections on each cell, the voltage response of all 8 cells was then separately and simultaneously obtained.

Figure 3D illustrates the steps from single EIS measurements to multi-channel and multi-frequency voltage response testing. It can be observed that multi-channel, multi-frequency measurements can enable the batch testing and evaluation of batteries within seconds. Theoretically, for multiple batteries, obtaining individual impedance is typically expressed as
(Equation 1)
Z j
( ω )
=
V j
( ω )
/
I j
( ω )
, j = 1 , 2 , … , N
I j
( ω )
represents the perturbation current for each EIS measurement, and
V j
( ω )
is measured over multiple cycles to improve signal quality and reduce noise. While this approach ensures accurate impedance calculation, it significantly increases total measurement time. By contrast, rapid EIS techniques superimpose multiple sine waves to create a composite excitation signal, allowing simultaneous perturbation of the system at several frequencies. 42 The corresponding voltage response is then decomposed to extract impedance at each frequency, enabling faster diagnostics while preserving frequency-domain information. In the case of multi-channel EIS, the expression simplifies to
(Equation 2)
Z j
( ω )
=
V j
( ω )
/ I
( ω )
, j = 1 , 2 , … , N
where
I
( ω )
is a single, shared excitation current applied across all channels, and
V j
( ω )
can be acquired simultaneously. Multi-channel methods can measure the impedance of multiple cells at the same time, but they still depend on time-to-frequency transformation, usually done by a microcontroller or digital signal processor, which adds cost and complexity as the number of channels increases. However, for our multi-channel and multi-frequency electrical excitation method:
(Equation 3)
V j
( ω )
=
Z j
( ω )
· I
( ω )
, j = 1 , 2 , … , N
where
I
( ω )
remains the same for all batteries, meaning the voltage response
V j
( ω )
inherently contains the impedance information of each battery. Unlike traditional methods, the voltage response
V j
( ω )
can be captured directly in the time domain, bypassing the need for a full EIS setup while preserving the critical impedance characteristics.

The key distinction of the MMER technique compared with rapid EIS lies in its direct use of raw voltage responses under a shared excitation current, without requiring algorithmic extraction of impedance values at each frequency point. By avoiding the transformation from the time domain to the frequency domain, which is typically handled by microcontrollers or digital signal processors in EIS systems, MMER reduces hardware complexity and avoids potential computational errors. This lowers system costs by removing the need for advanced processing units and relaxes strict requirements on sampling accuracy and phase alignment, improving scalability and robustness. MMER uses simultaneous analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) to record electrical responses in parallel, with minimal hardware. Instead of summing multiple sine waves, it applies a binary multi-frequency excitation signal derived from Walsh functions, which are suited to digital systems and can be efficiently generated using FPGAs, supporting real-time operation.

The actual performance of the batteries is shown in Figure 4 A, where it can be observed that the consistency between the batteries is generally good. At the end of the discharge, the highest voltage is approximately 2.86 V for cell #1, while the lowest voltage is ca. 2.80 V for cell #4, with a difference of only about 0.06 V between the highest and lowest voltages. In the locally enlarged view (Figure 4B), it is evident that the performance of cells #1 and #6 is slightly better, while cell #4 exhibits the worst performance. This observation does not fully align with the individual impedance spectra and multi-frequency voltage responses of the batteries in the previous section. This discrepancy is because, during multiple measurements, the electrochemical signals are highly sensitive to the connection between the battery and the holder (considering the internal resistance of the battery is in the milliohm range; see Figure 1A). Moreover, MMER applies a broadband multi-frequency perturbation to all cells simultaneously, cutting measurement time and thus preventing SoC drift and thermal shifts during the test. Because all cells have identical wiring, parasitic resistances and inductances are uniform across channels, aligning the MMER with the discharge curve. Conversely, separate multi-frequency voltage response does not lie in the tighter control of SoC, temperature, timing, and instrumentation uniformity inherent to parallel testing so that small experimental differences accumulate when tests are repeated.

The experimental setup for executing multi-frequency current excitation and acquiring multi-channel voltage responses is shown in Figure S11 (further details regarding the methods can be found in the Methods). The cell voltage responses obtained simultaneously using the multi-channel and multi-frequency technique are displayed in Figure 4C, with a measurement time of 1 s (one of the four repeat segments), whereas the same number of cells requires 8 s for individual multi-frequency voltage response measurements and approximately 10 min for separate EIS measurements. In our study, only eight cells were simultaneously measured; however, the advantage in diagnostic efficiency improvement becomes even more significant as the number of cells increases—with the relative ease of adding voltage response sensors, one can envisage testing every cell in a module or even a whole end-of-life (EoL) automotive pack using one, 1 s, current perturbation. In this way, high-throughput, rapid, but information-rich battery diagnostics could keep pace with the exponential rise in global battery numbers. The voltage response of cell #1 sampled by a single channel of the potentiostat and the voltage responses of each channel sampled are shown in Figure S12. The close match (Figure S12I) between the potentiostat-sampled and the in-house hardware-sampled voltage response for cell #1 validates the accuracy of the multi-channel voltage responses.

In the locally enlarged views in Figures 4E and 4F, it can be observed that cell #4 (Figure 4E) and cell #8 (Figure 4F) exhibit relatively higher amplitude multi-frequency voltage responses, with SDs of ca. 0.074 V and ca. 0.074 V, respectively. This is consistent with the discharge curves. Additionally, cell #1, which shows better performance in the discharge curve, also has the lowest amplitude in the AC voltage response, with an SD of ca. 0.064 V. From the normalized Euclidean distance matrix between different cells calculated based on multi-channel and multi-frequency voltage responses (Figure 4D), it is evident that cell #4 and cell #8 have prominent differences from the other cells. These two cells should be excluded when remanufacturing a second-life battery module using those 8 cells. Compared with multiple measurements of multi-frequency electrical signal responses, the multi-channel, multi-frequency technique enables batch testing. This has obvious applications for the triage of cells for second-life applications but could also be used to identify manufacturing inconsistencies in pristine cells and assess differences in batches from the same manufacturer.

Currently, a commercially available potentiostat is used to perform multi-frequency current excitation. However, in practical applications, to reduce costs, an alternative approach can be employed: using a signal generator to generate arbitrary waveforms point by point. The voltage-to-current conversion and amplification can then be achieved using a simple power supply. The voltage of each cell can be sampled with a low-cost ADC, enabling further cost reduction of the hardware. We estimate a significant cost reduction in hardware enabled by our technique (see Table S1 for an estimated cost breakdown based on prices at the time of writing) in scale up but also acknowledge that synchronizing multiple channels brings its own challenges. In real battery packs, individual cells are often configured in a combination of series and parallel connections. Under these circumstances, for multi-channel, multi-frequency voltage response signals, the unequal currents flowing into each cell make it challenging to analyze the responses solely based on amplitude. As shown in Figure 2D, the response of the same cell under different excitation amplitudes may exhibit proportional behavior. By employing similarity comparisons, responses corresponding to different excitation currents but the same cell should theoretically be classified as originating from the same cell. Similarly, for responses obtained from different cells under various excitations, more advanced signal processing and ML algorithms 33 should be utilized for next-generation battery consistency assessment, rapid performance detection, SOC estimation, and real-time BMSs, which constitute the focus of our next step of research.

This study presents the MMER technique as a fast, scalable, and effective alternative to EIS for battery diagnostics. By using a multi-channel and multi-frequency binary excitation based on Walsh functions, MMER enables simultaneous measurement of all cells under a shared excitation current, acquiring information-rich diagnostic signals in just 1 s. This approach bypasses complex impedance extraction and instead compares raw voltage responses that inherently reflect impedance characteristics due to the shared input. Achieving over 99% reduction in measurement time, MMER addresses the critical need for rapid testing in giga-scale battery manufacturing and second-life applications. Its FPGA-based signal generation and low hardware requirements make the system compact, cost-efficient, and suitable for embedded integration.

The architecture allows synchronous voltage sampling across all channels, eliminating errors from sequential testing or repeated setups. MMER has shown strong agreement between electrical responses and actual performance indicators like discharge profiles, enabling accurate identification of underperforming cells. With constant measurement time regardless of cell number, the method fits naturally into the serial connection structure of battery modules and directly addresses the inherent challenge of cell imbalance. Combined with ML, MMER provides a robust platform for quality assurance, SoH/SOC estimation, and onboard monitoring. We also demonstrate that it is possible to make MMER measurements during active discharging of the cell and obtain the same level of information as when at open-circuit voltage, paving the way for operando EIS-like measurements in batteries. Reduction of measurement time to 1 s means that the system is effectively unchanged during the operando measurement, even potentially at rates as high as 10C (a 6-minute charge, 1/360th of the capacity passed during measurements). This is not possible with conventional EIS or even reduced frequency range EIS, which requires a steady state (and therefore no operating current) during measurement. Its binary-compatible excitation and multi-channel design enhance industrial readiness, while its adaptability extends to a wide range of electrochemical systems, including fuel cells and electrolyzers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impedance and multi-frequency electrical excitation response
ce:section.ce:para: Each battery was discharged to 3 V for all electrical excitation unless otherwise stated. Impedance measurements were conducted using a Gamry Reference 3000 in galvanostatic mode, applying an AC of 200 mA. The frequency range was set from 10 kHz to 1 Hz, with data recorded at ten points per decade. After allowing the battery to rest for 60 s, a multi-frequency excitation signal spanning 2 to 512 Hz was applied. The Gamry Reference 3000 functioned as both the signal generator and the data acquisition device, with a sampling rate of 10 kHz. Following another 60-s rest period, a second multi-frequency excitation signal, ranging from 1 to 1,024 Hz, was applied.

For the MMER, the multi-frequency signal based on the Walsh function was synthesized by:
(Equation 4)
f
(
m , t
)
= K · S g n
(
∑
k = 1
m
S g n
(
sin ⁡
(
2 k
f 0
π t
)
)
)
In which m represents the number of primary harmonics in the synthesized multi-frequency perturbation signal, while
S g n
( x )
denotes the symbolic function, and
f 0
is the fundamental frequency. The multi-frequency excitation signal for 2 to 512 Hz is defined as
f
(
9 , t
)
, and the frequency of each principal harmonic meets
2
k − 1
f 0
(
k = 1 , 2 , 3 , … , 9
)
, where the base frequency
f 0
= 2 Hz. The multi-frequency excitation signal for 1 to 1,024 Hz is defined as
f
(
11 , t
)
, and the frequency of each principal harmonic meets
2
k − 1
f 0
(
k = 1 , 2 , 3 , … , 11
)
, where the base frequency
f 0
= 1 Hz. Too high of the proportional coefficient
K
will damage the battery, but too low will cause the voltage response to be overridden by noise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-channel electrical excitation response and discharge
ce:section.ce:para: A multi-channel, multi-frequency electrical excitation and response acquisition system was developed to simultaneously sample the AC perturbation signal and the individual voltage responses using NI9205 and NI9220 modules (National Instruments, USA). The perturbation duration and sampling rate were set to 4 s and 10 kHz, respectively. As all eight batteries were connected in series, the negative terminal of the battery module was connected to the counter lead, while the working lead was connected to the positive terminal on the opposite side. The Gamry Reference 3000 generated the AC perturbation across the entire module, passing through all the batteries, while the NI9205 and NI9220 modules individually recorded the voltage response of each cell. Concurrently, the potentiostat included one channel dedicated to voltage response acquisition, which was used to measure the response of cell #1. The data from the potentiostat were then compared with the measurements obtained from the NI instruments.

To verify the generalizability of the proposed method, a BioLogic BCS-815 battery cycler with a BH-1i cell holder was used to apply the perturbation and record the response. While the system allows a minimum acquisition time of 2 ms in galvanostatic mode, the focus of this study is to demonstrate the method’s effectiveness under practical, application-oriented conditions, where such high-speed measurement equipment may not be readily available.

The experiments primarily targeted EoL cylindrical cells featuring lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA) chemistry, extracted from a decommissioned Tesla Model S battery module. The 300 mA CCCV charge-discharge cycling and capacity of these EoL 18650 cells were evaluated using a BioLogic BCS-815 battery testing system.

LG M50 lithium ion batteries with a nominal capacity of 5 Ah at different aging states are also investigated. The cells feature a LixNiyMnzCo1-y−zO2 (NMC) cathode with a composition of 80% Ni, 10% Mn, and 10% Co, as well as a graphite-silicon anode.

The Euclidean distance 43 between two voltage responses x(t) and y(t) can be calculated using the following equation:
(Equation 5)
d =
∑
i = 1
N
(
x i
−
y i
)
2
where
x i
and
y i
are the sampled values of the two voltage responses at the i-th point, and N is the total number of data points in the signals. For two-dimensional impedance signals X(t)=(x1(t), x2(t)) and Y(t)=(y1(t), y2(t)), the Euclidean distance can be calculated as:
(Equation 6)
d =
∑
i = 1
N
(
(
x 1
i
−
y 1
i
)
2
+
(
x 2
i
−
y 2
i
)
2
)
To normalize Euclidean distance matrix A (of size m×m), each element of the matrix can be normalized to [0,1]. The equation is:
(Equation 7)
A
n o r m
(
i , j
)
=
A
(
i , j
)
−
A min
A max
−
A min
in which
A
(
i , j
)
is the element of the matrix at row
i
and column
j
,
A min
is the minimum value in the matrix
A
, and
A max
is the maximum value in the matrix
A
.
Further information and requests for resources should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Rhodri Jervis (rhodri.jervis@ucl.ac.uk).
This study did not generate new, unique reagents.
Any additional information required to reanalyze the data reported in this paper is available from the lead contact upon request.

This work was carried out with funding from the Faraday Institution (faraday.ac.uk; EP/S003053/1), grant numbers FIRG060, FIRG061, FIRG082, FIRG085, and FIRG086. P.R.S. was supported by the Department of Science, Innovation, and Technology (DSIT) and the Royal Academy of Engineering under the Chair in Emerging Technologies program (CiET1718/59). W.D. and P.R.S. acknowledge the support of the Oxford Martin School.

Conceptualization, S.Z. and R.J.; methodology, S.Z.; investigation, S.Z. and W.D.; data curation, S.Z., B.M., and W.D.; writing – original draft, S.Z.; writing – review & editing, S.Z., W.D., P.R.S., T.M., and R.J.; funding acquisition, R.J.; resources, T.M. and R.J.; supervision, P.R.S., T.M., and R.J.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2025.102099.
